name: Local Lens
tagline: Your neighborhood, filtered to what matters.
description: Local Lens turns the chaos of citywide headlines into a hyperlocal news feed built around the places you actually live, work, and commute through. Instead of dumping every breaking story on users, it maps news to specific streets, transit lines, schools, stores, and neighborhoods, then rewrites each item into a practical impact summary: what happened, who it affects, and what you should do next. It solves the common problem of news overload and relevance blindness, where people miss important local developments because they are buried under national coverage or sensational alerts. In daily life, the app helps users decide whether to take a different route, whether a school delay will affect pickup, or whether a nearby power outage is likely to disrupt dinner plans. A commuter might see a bridge closure summarized as a 12-minute delay on their usual bus route, while a parent gets a push notification that a park event is canceled due to air quality. What makes it unique is that it combines geolocation, schedule context, and plain-language civic translation to show only the news that has a real-world consequence for that person, not just what is technically nearby.
features: The app pinpoints news stories on an interactive local map so users can immediately see whether an event affects their home, work, school, or commute.
It converts complex articles into plain-language impact cards that explain what changed, who is affected, and what action, if any, users should take.
It learns each user’s routine, such as commute times and favorite places, to prioritize stories that matter at the exact moments they are most useful.
It sends configurable micro-alerts for disruptions like transit delays, road closures, weather warnings, and utility outages without overwhelming users with broad breaking-news spam.
It includes community-verified follow-ups so residents can confirm whether a report is real-time and add practical details like alternate entrances, service status, or safe routes.

Audience includes urban and suburban adults ages 20-50 who rely on transit, manage family schedules, or navigate busy neighborhoods and want practical local news without doomscrolling. It is especially useful for parents, commuters, renters, and small business owners who need fast, location-specific updates in context.
Sarah just finished a stressful meeting and wants to know whether a subway signal issue will delay her trip home, so she opens Local Lens to see an impact summary on her exact commute line.
Marcus is picking up his kids from school and gets a micro-alert that a nearby street has been closed for emergency repairs, along with a suggested alternate pickup route.

Alicia is opening her coffee shop on a rainy morning and checks Local Lens to see that a neighborhood water main break will reduce foot traffic on her block, helping her adjust staffing and inventory.
